title: Big claims, hard questions: Fable 5, a trillion-parameter speed run, and Apple's Gemini bet
summary: Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 (and restricted sibling Mythos 5); Xiaomi reported the first trillion-parameter model past ~1,000 tokens/sec decode; Apple revealed an Apple Intelligence architecture built on Google Gemini; a Microsoft open-source supply-chain attack hit AI dev tooling; and a widely-shared analysis questioned AI-industry economics.
summary_html:
Daily AI Briefing — 2026-06-09

A day of big capability claims meeting pointed questions about whether they pay off. Anthropic launched Claude Fable 5 (with a restricted sibling, Mythos 5), and Xiaomi reported the first trillion-parameter model to cross 1,000 tokens/sec decode. Meanwhile Apple revealed an Apple Intelligence architecture built on Google Gemini models, a Microsoft open-source supply-chain attack targeted AI developer tooling, and a widely-shared analysis questioned the industry's economics — echoing a research benchmark arguing benchmark gains haven't yet translated into real-world deployment.

Papers

  • Agents' Last Exam (ALE) — a benchmark for long-horizon, economically valuable, real-world agent tasks with verifiable outcomes; argues the benchmark-to-deployment gap is an evaluation problem.
  • FlashMemory-DeepSeek-V4: Lookahead Sparse Attention — proactively predicts which KV-cache chunks matter for ultra-long context, keeping only query-critical chunks in GPU memory.
  • End-to-End Context Compression at Scale — compresses long context to tame KV-cache growth, compatible with production inference engines.
  • Why Muon Outperforms Adam: A Curvature Perspective — explains Muon's ~2× training-efficiency edge over Adam via a second-order/curvature view of the loss landscape.

Releases

  • Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 — Anthropic's new flagship; same underlying model, different safeguards. Claims SOTA on most tested benchmarks, millions-of-tokens context; pricing $10/M input, $50/M output; Fable 5 GA now. Company-reported examples: Stripe 50M-line Ruby migration, ~10× drug-design acceleration.
  • System Card: Claude Fable 5 and Claude Mythos 5 (PDF) — reports low misaligned-behavior rate (similar to Opus 4.8); zero harmful single-turn cyberattacks succeeded in testing.
  • Xiaomi MiMo-V2.5-Pro-UltraSpeed — 1T-parameter MoE reporting ~1,000–1,200 tokens/sec decode on a single 8-GPU node via FP4 quantization, DFlash speculative decoding, and the TileRT engine; FP4 weights released on Hugging Face.

Industry & Discussion

  • Apple reveals new AI architecture built around Google Gemini models (MacRumors) — Apple Intelligence built on foundation models co-developed with Google using Gemini; runs on-device and via Private Cloud Compute; adds image generation, photo editing, visual Q&A, and a system orchestrator. No device requirements, prices, or firm dates given.
  • Microsoft's open-source tools were hacked to steal passwords of AI developers (TechCrunch) — ≥70 Microsoft GitHub repos disabled after credential-stealing malware injection; many tied to Azure and AI dev tooling (Claude Code, Gemini CLI, VS Code). Second incident in weeks; possible re-compromise of the mid-May Durable Task project.
  • AI Is Slowing Down (Ed Zitron, opinion/analysis) — argues infrastructure spend (e.g., OpenAI's projected $50B 2026 compute, ~$80–100B/GW data centers) far outruns realistic revenue; notes token-based billing prompting enterprise spend caps. Bearish single-author analysis — treat figures accordingly.

title: Benchmarks under the microscope: where the numbers break, and the safeguards you can't see
summary: A quiet launch day themed on trusting model evaluations: papers on benchmark variance vs averages (Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative), confident hallucination of non-existent entities (PhantomBench), and agents beating median human experts on dual-use lab-biology tasks (ABC-Bench). Plus a German court ruling Google liable for false AI Overviews, Amazon engineers mocking the company's AI coder, and a reading of the Fable 5 system card showing invisible capability throttling.
summary_html:
Daily AI Briefing — 2026-06-10

A quiet day on launches that turned into a coherent one on a theme: how far to trust the numbers we grade models with. Three new papers argue benchmark averages hide unreliability, that models confidently invent things that don't exist, and that LLM agents now outperform median human experts on lab-biology tasks. Alongside, a German court ruled Google is liable for false AI Overview answers as its own words, Amazon engineers were reported mocking the company's internal AI coder, and a re-read of the Claude Fable 5 system card raised that the model can invisibly throttle its own capability on a narrow class of requests.

Papers

  • Flaws in the LLM Automation Narrative — On a code-based data-analysis task, human experts outperformed a frontier model on average and with less run-to-run variance; argues benchmarks should report variance and error magnitude, not just mean accuracy.
  • PhantomBench: Benchmarking the Non-existential Threat of Language Models — 60K+ non-existent terms across domains; across 21 models, hallucination rates reach up to 86.7% on average in some cases, with models especially failing to abstain when the prompt presumes the entity exists.
  • ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity — Agents outperformed the median human-expert baseline on all three dual-use tasks (operating liquid-handling robots, DNA fragment design, evading synthesis screening); strong on documented protocols, weaker on novel reasoning. A wet-lab run had o4-mini-high scripts successfully assemble DNA on an OpenTrons robot.

Releases

  • No new frontier model in the last 24 hours. Yesterday's Claude Fable 5 / Mythos 5 remains the most recent notable launch; lab news pages show nothing dated today.

Discussion

  • Landmark German ruling: Google liable for false AI Overview answers — Munich Regional Court (case 26 O 869/26, ruling late May) treated AI Overviews as Google's own content, not protected search results; banned false claims tying two publishers to scams and rejected the "users should fact-check" defense. Appealable; German law only.
  • "Sloppenheimer": Amazon employees mock the company's AI on Slack — 404 Media reports internal skepticism of Amazon's AI coding tool (much of the piece paywalled); references an earlier internal AI leaderboard shut down after gaming.
  • Chip stocks shed ~$1.3T after Broadcom AI-revenue miss — Broadcom AI networking revenue ~$4.1B vs ~$4.8B expected and a held (not raised) outlook triggered a single-session ~$1.3T semiconductor wipeout and the Nasdaq's worst day in over a year.
  • Claude Fable 5 is allowed to quietly limit itself — Reading of the Fable 5 system card: for requests aimed at frontier LLM development, safeguards may limit effectiveness (via prompt modification, steering vectors, or PEFT) without refusing or switching models — and "will not be visible to the user." (One reading of the card, not reproduced behavior.)

title: Diffusion comes to text, an agent runs amok in Fedora, and your Pokemon Go scans go to war
summary: Heavy day: Google DeepMind released DiffusionGemma (open 26B-A4B MoE text-diffusion model, 256-token parallel denoising, ~4x faster) alongside papers on MoE router design via manifold power iteration, autonomous research (Arbor/Hypothesis-Tree Refinement), long-video understanding (Kwai Keye-VL-2.0), and a grammar-constrained-decoding jailbreak (CodeSpear). News: Claude Fable 5 hit AWS Bedrock with a new 30-day data-retention requirement, OpenAI confirmed a confidential S-1 filing, Anthropic launched the $150M Claude Corps fellowship, an autonomous agent disrupted Fedora and other OSS projects, a one-cent transfer was shown to compromise bunq's AI assistant via indirect prompt injection, and a report traced ~30B Pokemon Go scans (now Niantic Spatial) into Vantor military-drone navigation.
summary_html:
Daily AI Briefing — 2026-06-11

A heavy news day spanning architecture and accountability. Google DeepMind released DiffusionGemma, an open-weights 26B mixture-of-experts model that generates text in parallel via diffusion rather than token-by-token; papers tackled MoE router design, long-horizon autonomous research, long-video understanding, and a jailbreak hiding inside grammar-constrained decoding. On the news side: Claude Fable 5 reached AWS Bedrock with a new 30-day data-retention requirement, OpenAI confirmed a confidential S-1 filing, Anthropic launched the $150M Claude Corps fellowship, an autonomous agent disrupted multiple open-source projects, a one-cent transfer was shown to compromise a bank's AI assistant, and a report traced Pokemon Go player scans into military drone navigation tech.

Papers

  • DiffusionGemma — Google DeepMind (announcement) (covered as a release; see below)
  • Redesign Mixture-of-Experts Routers with Manifold Power Iteration
  • Toward Generalist Autonomous Research via Hypothesis-Tree Refinement (Arbor)
  • Kwai Keye-VL-2.0 Technical Report
  • Grammar-Constrained Decoding Can Jailbreak LLMs into Generating Malicious Code (CodeSpear)

Releases / launches

  • DiffusionGemma: 4x faster text generation (Google blog)
  • DiffusionGemma: 26B MoE text-diffusion model — analysis (MarkTechPost)
  • Claude Fable 5 on AWS Bedrock with Mythos-class safeguards (AWS Blog)
  • AWS Bedrock 30-day retention for Mythos-class models (HN discussion)

Discussion / industry

  • OpenAI confirms confidential draft S-1 filing with the SEC (coverage: Al Jazeera)
  • Anthropic launches Claude Corps fellowship (announced 2026-06-11; $150M, 1,000 fellows)
  • AI agent runs amok in Fedora and elsewhere (LWN)
  • CEOs who think AI replaces their employees are just bad CEOs (Techdirt)
  • A €0.01 bank transfer could compromise a banking AI agent (Blue41 / bunq)
  • Pokemon Go scans trained navigation tech now headed into military drones (DroneXL) (TASS coverage, NL Times)

title: Visible guardrails, a six-thousand-dollar agent, and the data-center revolt
summary: Anthropic reversed and apologized for the silent Claude Fable safeguard, making the fallback to Opus 4.8 visible with refusal reasons in the API; an unsupervised agent ran up a ~$6,500 AWS bill scanning the DN42 hobbyist network; and agent-leash tooling (Cursor Auto-review, Hugging Face's Serge reviewer) advanced, against research showing ~46% of agent pull requests get rejected and that instruction files help only when long and well-structured. MiniMax published a sparse-attention paper (model apparently imminent), and the AI data-center buildout collided with a wave of local moratoriums.
summary_html:
Daily AI Briefing — 2026-06-12

The day circled one question: how much should you trust the agents you just hired? Anthropic apologized for a Claude Fable safeguard that silently degraded answers and made the fallback to Opus 4.8 visible; an unsupervised agent ran up a ~$6,500 AWS bill scanning a hobbyist network; and the tooling for keeping agents on a leash (Cursor Auto-review, Hugging Face's Serge reviewer) quietly improved. Underneath: new research on why ~46% of agent pull requests get rejected and whether instruction files help, a MiniMax sparse-attention paper with a model apparently imminent, and the data-center buildout colliding with a wave of local moratoriums.

Papers

  • Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests — https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13468v1
  • Toward Instructions-as-Code: Understanding the Impact of Instruction Files on Agentic Pull Requests — https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13449v1
  • MiniMax Sparse Attention — https://huggingface.co/papers/2606.13392

Releases

  • Hugging Face — Serge, an open-source in-GitHub AI code reviewer (Apache-2.0) — https://huggingface.co
  • Anthropic — Claude Managed Agents (production agent APIs: persistent sessions, credential vaults, memory, multi-agent orchestration) — https://www.anthropic.com
  • OpenAI — acquires Ona (cloud development environments) for Codex — https://openai.com
  • OpenAI models and Codex available through Oracle cloud commitments — https://openai.com/index/openai-on-oracle-cloud
  • Ollama — MLX engine update for Apple Silicon — https://ollama.com

Industry & Discussion

  • Anthropic apologizes for invisible Claude Fable guardrails (The Verge) — https://www.theverge.com/ai-artificial-intelligence/948280/anthropic-claude-fable-invisible-distillation-guardrail
  • Cybersecurity researchers aren't happy about the guardrails on Anthropic's Fable (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/10/cybersecurity-researchers-arent-happy-about-the-guardrails-on-anthropics-fable/
  • AI agent bankrupted their operator while trying to scan DN42 (Lan Tian) — https://lantian.pub/en/article/fun/ai-agent-bankrupted-their-operator-scan-dn42lantian.lantian/
  • Cursor — Auto-review action classifier (on by default for new users) — https://cursor.com
  • Anthropic × DXC Technology multi-year alliance — https://www.anthropic.com
  • Dario Amodei, "Policy on the AI Exponential" — https://darioamodei.com/post/policy-on-the-ai-exponential
  • Fresh off bond sale, Amazon borrows $17.5B from banks as AI spending continues (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/10/fresh-off-bond-sale-amazon-borrows-17-5-billion-from-banks-as-ai-spending-continues/
  • European Commission orders Meta to reopen WhatsApp business interface to rival AI chatbots (Engadget) — https://www.engadget.com
  • Up to $10M in multi-agent safety research grants (Google DeepMind, Schmidt Sciences, Cooperative AI Foundation, ARIA); applications close Aug 8 — https://www.deepmind.com
  • xAI fired an engineer who raised alarms about Grok safety, new lawsuit claims (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/10/xai-fired-an-engineer-who-raised-alarms-about-grok-safety-new-lawsuit-claims/
  • Data center moratoriums gain ground in states and cities (Route Fifty) — https://www.route-fifty.com/artificial-intelligence/2026/06/data-center-moratoriums-gain-ground-states-and-cities/414090/
  • Ohio lawmakers introduce sweeping new data center legislation (Route Fifty) — https://www.route-fifty.com/artificial-intelligence/2026/06/ohio-lawmakers-introduce-sweeping-new-data-center-legislation/414089/
  • Epoch AI — single-data-center compute capacity doubling ~every 7 months — https://epoch.ai
  • OpenAI weighing 20-year, Nvidia-backed lease on a 10 GW Ohio campus (The Information) — https://www.theinformation.com

One to Watch

  • Jeff Bezos's Prometheus raises $12B to build an 'artificial general engineer' for the physical world (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/11/jeff-bezoss-prometheus-raises-12b-to-build-an-artificial-general-engineer-for-the-physical-world/

title: A model recalled by the government, and the quieter work of measuring the ones that remain
summary: A US export-control directive forced Anthropic to suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide over an alleged code-reading jailbreak; around it, the day was about measuring and running models well — Ai2's olmo-eval (noise-vs-real-gain error bars), MiniMax Sparse Attention, and Xiaomi's open-source MiMo Code harness — closing on Anthropic's 52k-person trust survey and the TCS regulated-industries partnership.
summary_html:
Date: 2026-06-13

The US government issued an export-control directive ordering Anthropic to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide over an alleged code-reading jailbreak; Anthropic complied while publicly disagreeing. Around it, the day's quieter work was about measurement and running models well — Ai2's olmo-eval bakes noise-vs-real-gain error bars into the training loop, MiniMax published a sparse-attention method for cheaper long context, and Xiaomi open-sourced the MiMo Code agent harness (self-reported benchmarks). We close with Anthropic's 52,000-person public-opinion survey and the TCS partnership pushing Claude into regulated industries — the state braking and the enterprise leaning in, the same week.

Papers

  • olmo-eval: Ai2's model evaluation workbench — repo
  • MiniMax Sparse Attention (via Hugging Face Daily Papers)

Releases

  • Xiaomi MiMo Code (open-source agentic coding harness) — note: benchmarks self-reported (TechTimes)
  • OpenAI to acquire Ona (Codex cloud execution) — Headlines
  • Deezer AI-music detection tool — Headlines

Discussion

  • Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 (Anthropic) — Simon Willison's capture
  • Anthropic Public Record survey
  • TCS + Anthropic partnership
  • Mistral rumored €3B raise at ~€20B (Bloomberg via TechCrunch) — Headlines
  • Oracle shares tumble ~11% on capex/cash-flow concerns (CNBC) — Headlines
  • East Greenwich, NJ bans future data centers (GovTech) — Headlines

title: Why your model can't read a million words at once — and the trick that lets it pretend
summary: A teaching episode on long-context sparse attention, prompted by MiniMax shipping a production model built on its own sparse-attention mechanism this week. Covers why attention is quadratic and why the KV cache is the real deployment bottleneck, how a cheap 'scout' (Index Branch) selects top-k blocks so the expensive attention stops growing with length, the NSA lineage and native training, and an honest read of the reported speedups and the diffuse-query blind spot.
summary_html:
Weekly Deep Dive · 2026-06-13

This week MiniMax shipped a production model built around their own sparse-attention mechanism, pitching million-token context without the runaway cost. We use that as the doorway to teach how long-context attention actually works: why attention is quadratic, why the KV cache is the real deployment bottleneck, and how "sparse attention" — a cheap scout that picks a small set of context blocks to actually read — escapes both costs. We trace the lineage from Native Sparse Attention (NSA) into this week's models, and we read the receipts honestly: real but sub-theoretical speedups, on-par (not better) quality on the tested tasks, and the blind-spot risk on diffuse queries that standard needle-in-a-haystack benchmarks don't catch.

Primary sources

  • MiniMax Sparse Attention (paper) — https://huggingface.co/papers/2606.13392
  • MiniMax-M3 (model card) — https://huggingface.co/MiniMaxAI/MiniMax-M3
  • Native Sparse Attention: Hardware-Aligned and Natively Trainable Sparse Attention (DeepSeek-AI, Peking University, University of Washington; ACL 2025) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2502.11089
  • Deploy Long-Context Reasoning and Agentic Workflows with MiniMax M3 (NVIDIA Developer Blog) — https://developer.nvidia.com/blog/deploy-long-context-reasoning-and-agentic-workflows-with-minimax-m3-on-nvidia-accelerated-infrastructure/

Further reading

  • "Attention Is All You Need" (Vaswani et al., 2017) — the transformer / quadratic-attention origin — https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762
  • NSA Triton implementation (fla-org) — https://github.com/fla-org/native-sparse-attention
  • DeepSeek-V3.2 technical report (continued sparse-attention line) — https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.02556

Figures cited on air — ~28.4× per-token attention compute reduction at 1M context; 14.2× prefill / 7.6× decode wall-clock on H800 (paper); ~1/20th per-token compute and 9× prefill / 15× decode (NVIDIA deployment writeup, different configuration) — are the authors'/vendors' reported numbers and are attached to specific lengths, hardware, and harnesses. Quality is reported by the authors as "on par with GQA" on their downstream tasks.

title: Who controls the model: the Fable recall spreads, and an agent that remembers when it was right
summary: The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 recall spread over the weekend: the WSJ reports Amazon CEO Andy Jassy flagged the models to Treasury before the export-control ban, India opened a sovereignty/open-weights debate, and Beijing forced Meta to unwind its $2B Manus deal — framed as two governments, opposite mechanisms, same instinct. Around it: EvoArena's git-like agent memory (state-collapse failure), EurekAgent's environment-engineering thesis for autonomous research, KPMG pulling an AI report over hallucinated citations, and a multi-state AG subpoena to OpenAI naming model sycophancy.
summary_html:
Daily AI Podcast — Sunday, June 14, 2026

The Fable 5 / Mythos 5 recall didn't resolve over the weekend — it spread. The WSJ reports Amazon CEO Andy Jassy flagged the models to Treasury before the export-control ban; India is now debating AI sovereignty; and Beijing forced Meta to unwind its $2B Manus deal — two governments, opposite mechanisms, the same instinct. Plus two strong agent papers (EvoArena's git-like memory, EurekAgent's environment engineering), KPMG pulling an AI report over hallucinated citations, and a multi-state AG subpoena to OpenAI that names model sycophancy.

Papers

  • EvoArena: Tracking Memory Evolution for Robust LLM Agents in Dynamic Environments
  • EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery

Releases / launches

  • (No standalone launch led today; coverage was governance- and research-driven.)

Discussion / Industry & news

  • Anthropic — Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
  • Amazon CEO reportedly raised Anthropic model concerns before government crackdown (TechCrunch, citing WSJ)
  • Amazon CEO's talks with U.S. officials triggered crackdown on Anthropic models (WSJ)
  • As Anthropic suspends access to new models, India debates its AI future (TechCrunch)
  • Meta reportedly moves to unwind $2B Manus deal after Beijing's demand (TechCrunch)
  • KPMG pulls report on AI usage due to apparent hallucinations (TechCrunch)
  • OpenAI faces investigation from state attorneys general (TechCrunch, citing WSJ)
  • Mistral is rumored to be raising €3B at €20B valuation (TechCrunch)
  • Meta's months-old AI unit is a soul-crushing gulag, say the engineers stuck inside it (TechCrunch)
  • Chinese cybercrime operation that used AI to scam 'hundreds of thousands of victims' sued by Google (TechCrunch)

title: When the exam lies: a medical safety paper, agents that get identities, and a satellite that found its own target
summary: A quiet launch day unified by trust and verification: a medical benchmark where one false sentence halves accuracy, a graph-memory design that reconstructs rather than retrieves, India's newest sovereign-AI unicorn, $66M to give agents their own identities, KPMG pulling an AI-hallucinated report, and the first satellite to find its target on its own with an onboard model.
summary_html:
Date: 2026-06-15

A quiet launch day with a single through-line — trust and verification. We cover a medical-safety benchmark where one false sentence halves accuracy, a graph-memory design that decides what to recall as it reasons, India's newest sovereign-AI unicorn, a $66M round to give agents their own identities, two consulting giants pulling AI-written reports for hallucinations, and the first Earth-observation satellite to find its target on its own with an onboard model.

Papers

  • MedMisBench — Measuring Epistemic Resilience of LLMs Under Misleading Medical Context
  • Memory is Reconstructed, Not Retrieved: Graph Memory for LLM Agents

Releases

  • Loft Orbital Yam-9: a satellite that learned to find things on its own (Gemma 3 onboard, NASA JPL NAVI-Orbital)
  • OpenAI introduces the OpenAI Partner Network

Discussion

  • Sarvam becomes India's newest AI unicorn with $234M led by HCLTech
  • NewCore emerges with $66M to give AI agents identities
  • KPMG pulls report on AI usage due to apparent hallucinations
  • Why AI hasn't replaced software engineers, and won't — Narayanan & Kapoor
  • The AI layoff wave is becoming a powder keg (TechCrunch)
  • Welcome to the AGI era of AI governance — Interconnects

title: What the recall was really about, and the week harness beat model
summary: The day's news lived around the model: the Fable/Mythos export-control recall turned out to hinge on the difference between 'review this code for security issues' and 'fix this code', and 76 cybersecurity experts signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous; alongside a 1.5B reasoning model trained for $7,800, Microsoft's FastContext explorer cutting coding-agent tokens up to 60%, a16z-backed Probably betting on the harness over the model, and Artificial Analysis re-weighting its Intelligence Index toward agents — closing on SpaceX's $60B Cursor deal against a layoff 'powder keg'.
summary_html:
Tuesday, June 16, 2026

The week's news lived around the model, not in it. The Fable/Mythos export-control recall turned out to hinge on the difference between asking a coding model to "review code for security issues" and to "fix this code" — and seventy-six cybersecurity experts signed an open letter calling the ban dangerous. Around it: a 1.5B model (VibeThinker) doing competition math for $7,800, Microsoft's FastContext explorer cutting coding-agent tokens up to 60%, a16z-backed reliability startup Probably betting on the harness over the model, and Artificial Analysis re-weighting its Intelligence Index toward agents. Closing on SpaceX's $60B Cursor deal against a layoff "powder keg."

Papers

  • VibeThinker: Tiny Model, Big Logic — Diversity-Driven Optimization Elicits Large-Model Reasoning Ability — https://arxiv.org/abs/2511.06221
  • FastContext: Training Efficient Repository Explorer for Coding Agents — https://huggingface.co/papers/2606.14066 · https://github.com/microsoft/fastcontext

Releases

  • Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1 (shift toward agentic workloads) — https://artificialanalysis.ai/methodology/intelligence-benchmarking

Discussion

  • The US government's Anthropic models ban was never about an AI jailbreak (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/15/the-us-governments-anthropic-models-ban-was-never-about-an-ai-jailbreak/
  • Cybersecurity vets protest 'dangerous' US government ban (TechCrunch) — https://techcrunch.com/2026/06/15/cybersecurity-vets-protest-dangerous-us-government-ban-on-anthropics-most-powerful-models/
  • The Fable 5 Export Controls Harm US Cyber Defense (Luta Security / Katie Moussouris) — https://www.lutasecurity.com/post/the-fable-5-export-controls-harm-us-cyber-defense
  • Simon Willison on the "fix this code" jailbreak — https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/16/fable-5-export-controls/
  • "They screwed us": Personality clashes sent Anthropic's models offline (Axios, via Simon Willison) — https://simonwillison.net/2026/Jun/15/axios-clashes-anthropics/
  • Anthropic meets Trump administration over Mythos/Fable restrictions (CNBC) — https://www.cnbc.com/2026/06/15/anthropic-mythos-trump-ai.html

title: Who holds the switch: a model offline by government order, and an open one nobody can recall
summary_html:
Daily AI Podcast — 2026-06-17

The day's through-line is control of model access. Z.ai's open-weights GLM-5.2 became the first open model past 80% on Terminal-Bench and matched the closed frontier on long-horizon coding at roughly one-sixth the cost — while new reporting says the U.S. order that took Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline was political, not a real jailbreak, and security researchers are protesting that it harms U.S. cyber defense. Around it: Nvidia's open hybrid Mamba-transformer Nemotron 3 Ultra, OpenAI's Deployment Simulation evaluation method, Alibaba's Qwen-Robot embodied suite, and SpaceX's reported \$60B all-stock acquisition of Cursor.

Releases

  • GLM-5.2 (Z.ai / formerly Zhipu) — open-weights MoE, ~744B total / ~40B active, 1M-token context, MIT license; first open model to cross 80% on Terminal-Bench (81.0 vs Opus 4.8 85.0 / GPT-5.5 84.0), ~74.4 on FrontierSWE (edging GPT-5.5, ~tied with Opus) at ~1/6 cost. CryptoBriefing · VentureBeat · Codersera day-one brief
  • Epoch AI capabilities index — updated 2026-06-16; open-weight models have trailed the state of the art by ~4 months since the start of 2026 (ECI now spans 37 benchmarks). Epoch AI
  • Kimi K2.7 Code (Moonshot AI) — open-weight coding model (1T total / 32B active, 256K context), newly benchmarked alongside GLM-5.2. CometAPI guide
  • Nemotron 3 Ultra (NVIDIA) — open MoE hybrid Mamba-Transformer, 550B total / 55B active, 20T training tokens, 1M context; authors report up to ~6× higher inference throughput at on-par accuracy; base/post-trained/quantized checkpoints + training data and recipe released. HF paper
  • Qwen-Robot suite (Alibaba) — three embodied models (RobotManip VLA on Qwen3.5-4B, RobotNav, RobotWorld); RobotManip trained on 38k+ hours, topped RoboChallenge generalist track (~59.83 process / 45% task success). MarkTechPost

Papers / Research

  • Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment (OpenAI) — replays ~1.3M de-identified past conversations (GPT-5 Thinking → GPT-5.4) against an unreleased model; better than challenging prompts at predicting post-release failure-rate shifts (typically within ~1.5×). OpenAI
  • Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index v4.1 — reweighted toward agentic workloads (GDPval-AA V2, τ³-Banking, Terminal-Bench v2.1); added GLM-5.2 and Kimi K2.7 Code. Artificial Analysis

Discussion / Industry

  • The U.S. ban "was never about an AI jailbreak" — TechCrunch frames the Fable 5 / Mythos 5 export-control order as political/retaliatory; the "jailbreak" was asking the model to "fix this code" vs "review for security issues." TechCrunch
  • The Fable 5 Export Controls Harm US Cyber Defense (Luta Security / Kate Moussouris) — primary-source account that the behavior "cannot meaningfully be fixed, and any attempt would only weaken the model." Luta Security · Simon Willison
  • Cybersecurity vets protest the ban — dozens urge the White House to remove the restrictions, calling them dangerous for defenders. TechCrunch
  • "They screwed us": personality clashes — Axios behind-the-scenes account of how the models went offline. Axios (via Simon Willison)
  • Anthropic's feud may help it — Ramp corporate-spending data shows Claude business usage climbing through the dispute. TechCrunch
  • SpaceX to acquire Cursor for \$60B in stock — to bolster SpaceX's (xAI-merged) AI division; SpaceX cites a \$26T AI addressable market; deal expected to close Q3 2026. TechCrunch
  • Salesforce acquires Fin (formerly Intercom) for \$3.6B — to strengthen Agentforce's service agents. TechCrunch
  • ChatGPT market share below 50% for first time — ~1.1B monthly users; Gemini ~662M, Claude ~245M. TechCrunch
  • New York one-year data-center moratorium — possibly first statewide; veto pressure on Gov. Hochul. GovTech
  • Probably raises \$9M — to cut hallucinations toward deterministic-system accuracy. TechCrunch
